primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cellulitis of the right lower limb with systemic inflammatory response (portal of entry: insect bite)

pathophysiology_summary: Bacteria — most commonly beta-haemolytic streptococci and Staphylococcus aureus — enter the dermis and subcutaneous tissue through a break in the skin barrier (here, an insect bite on the shin) and spread through the soft tissues, producing an expanding, poorly demarcated area of erythema, warmth, oedema and tenderness. Superficial dermal oedema produces the peau d'orange appearance, and lymphatic involvement produces tender regional (inguinal) lymphadenopathy. Release of bacterial products and cytokines drives a systemic inflammatory response — fever, rigors, tachycardia and malaise — which signals a more severe infection (approximately Eron class II: systemically unwell but no unstable comorbidity) at risk of progressing to bacteraemia and sepsis. The critical differential is necrotising fasciitis, a surgical emergency in which infection tracks along fascial planes; its hallmarks (pain out of proportion, violaceous bullae, skin necrosis/anaesthesia, crepitus, rapid systemic collapse) are screened for and, in this case, absent — but necrotising infection cannot be definitively excluded without in-person assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly spreading unilateral erythema, warmth, tenderness and swelling with systemic fever and a skin-break portal of entry.
A spreading soft-tissue infection with systemic toxicity mandates an active screen for necrotising features; it is the can't-miss surgical emergency behind this presentation.
Unilateral hot, swollen, painful leg — but high fever, rigors, demarcated spreading erythema and a clear cutaneous portal favour infection over venous occlusion.
Can mimic red lower-leg skin, but is typically chronic, bilateral and non-febrile — the acute unilateral onset with high fever argues against it.

differential.diagnosis: acute contact dermatitis or insect-bite hypersensitivity reaction
differential.evidence_basis: An itchy local reaction to a bite is common, but does not produce rapidly ascending erythema with rigors and systemic toxicity — treating this as an allergic rash is the anchoring trap.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading diagnosis is a spreading soft-tissue infection; the mandatory parallel track is the necrotising-fasciitis screen. Do NOT settle on a benign dermatitis/bite-reaction explanation.

differential.diagnosis: cellulitis of the lower limb with systemic inflammatory response
differential.evidence_basis: Classic infective constellation plus systemic toxicity (fever, rigors, tachycardia); necrotising features screened negative but not excludable remotely.
No crepitus, violaceous bullae, cutaneous anaesthesia or pain out of proportion reported — lowers probability, but a negative remote screen cannot exclude it; keeps in-person assessment mandatory.
Still possible / can coexist, but the febrile, toxic, spreading-erythema picture with a portal of entry is dominated by infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen lowers but does not eliminate necrotising fasciitis; the correct posture is urgent in-person assessment with explicit necrotising/sepsis safety-netting, not remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: cellulitis of the right lower limb with systemic inflammatory response
differential.evidence_basis: Insect-bite portal, rapidly ascending unilateral erythema, warmth, oedema, peau d'orange, tender inguinal lymphadenopathy, and systemic toxicity — a systemically unwell cellulitis needing prompt systemic antibiotics and in-person review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth end-point is a presumptive diagnosis of a systemically-unwell spreading soft-tissue infection requiring urgent same-day in-person medical assessment for antibiotics and to exclude necrotising infection — not a confirmed remote diagnosis and not home self-care.

red_flags.description: Rapidly ascending unilateral erythema (ankle to mid-calf within ~12 hours) from a skin-break portal of entry
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid spread signals an aggressive soft-tissue infection at risk of proximal progression and bacteraemia; the rate of spread also raises the necrotising-fasciitis question.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treated spreading infection progresses to extensive cellulitis, abscess, bacteraemia or sepsis.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity — high fever (38.8), rigors and tachycardia (systemic inflammatory response)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Two or more SIRS features with a soft-tissue source mark an Eron class II (or higher) infection and impending sepsis — an indication for urgent in-person assessment and consideration of IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed sepsis; delay in escalation and source control leads to septic shock and organ dysfunction.

red_flags.description: Necrotising-fasciitis screen — pain out of proportion, violaceous bullae, skin necrosis/anaesthesia, crepitus
red_flags.clinical_significance: These features would convert the presentation from an urgent medical problem to an immediate surgical emergency (T5); their absence lowers but does not exclude necrotising infection, which cannot be ruled out remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed necrotising fasciitis has very high mortality; failing to screen for it, or falsely excluding it remotely, is the catastrophic error.

red_flags.description: Progression to shock — dizziness/pre-syncope on standing, confusion, or reduced urine output
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks the transition from systemic inflammatory response to septic shock; if present, escalates to an immediate 000 emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised septic shock; preventable death from delayed resuscitation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading a red, itchy-looking leg with a recent insect bite as contact dermatitis or a bite hypersensitivity reaction and advising a topical steroid or antihistamine — missing a spreading bacterial infection and delaying systemic antibiotics.

Treating the skin in isolation and under-triaging to routine care despite fever, rigors and tachycardia, which mark a systemically-unwell (Eron class II+) infection needing same-day in-person assessment.

Being reassured by a negative remote red-flag screen and reassuring the patient at home — necrotising infection cannot be excluded without in-person assessment, and early necrotising fasciitis can look like ordinary cellulitis.

Attempting to prescribe/advise oral antibiotics and manage at home over chat instead of arranging urgent in-person assessment — Breath-Ezy cannot examine the limb, mark the border, or safely start antibiotics remotely for a systemically unwell patient.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate (clinician-measured: BP 110/70, HR 112, RR 20, SpO2 97%)
Palpated warmth, exact extent and demarcation of erythema, and whether crepitus is present
Reliable exclusion of necrotising fasciitis or a deep-space infection
Inflammatory markers, white cell count, blood cultures and any imaging (e.g. ultrasound to exclude DVT/abscess)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a systemically-unwell spreading soft-tissue infection and arrange URGENT same-day in-person medical assessment (T4 — emergency department or urgent same-day GP/urgent care) for examination, blood tests, and commencement of antibiotics (oral vs IV decided in person); provide explicit necrotising-fasciitis and sepsis safety-netting with an immediate 000 pathway if red flags appear. Breath-Ezy does not prescribe or administer antibiotics.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the infective picture is fairly classic, but the real risk is UNDER-action: reassuring a benign rash, substituting a topical steroid/antihistamine, or arranging only routine review, thereby delaying systemic antibiotics and missing early sepsis or necrotising infection.
